FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=10,588, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, mixed-effects models on ABCD data across 2-year, 3-year, and 4-year follow-up waves.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: three mixed-effects logistic regression analyses testing smartphone ownership and age of first smartphone acquisition with depression, obesity, and insufficient sleep outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Pediatrics (2026); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: smartphone ownership (yes/no) as IV paired with depression (OR=1.31), obesity (OR=1.40), and insufficient sleep (OR=1.62) as individual-trait outcomes (Table 1 in main paper). Age of first smartphone acquisition paired with obesity (OR=1.09 per year earlier) and sleep (OR=1.08). Acquiring smartphone in past year paired with clinical-level psychopathology (OR=1.57) and insufficient sleep (OR=1.50). All outcomes are individual-trait-level health constructs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple ORs with 95% CIs reported throughout the paper for smartphone ownership/acquisition vs. health outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses conducted: mixed-effects logistic regression with ORs, 95% CIs, and p-values reported for all three analyses.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone is the IV (not DV) in all analyses. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere — that condition is not met here. EC7 is irrelevant.
notes: Clean INCLUDE. Three analyses: (1) smartphone ownership at age 12 vs. health outcomes at age 12 (cross-sectional); (2) age of first acquisition vs. health outcomes among owners; (3) acquiring smartphone in past year vs. outcomes at next wave (longitudinal). Supplemental tables S3-S22 provide additional breakouts. MPIQ used only as sensitivity exclusion covariate, not as primary exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: Study analyzes N=11,875 children from the ABCD Study (ABCD 5.1 data release, https://doi.org/10.15154/z563-zd24), baseline ages 9-10, 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal linear mixed models and single-mediation analyses (lavaan) testing neurodevelopmental spectrum at age 10 predicting psychiatric dimensions at ages 11 and 12, with environmental and lifestyle factors as mediators.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in JAACAP Open (advance online 2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use variables ("social media," "passive watching," "active gaming," "social engagement") appear ONLY as candidate mediators in mediation models linking the neurodevelopmental spectrum (IV) to psychiatric dimensions (DV). In Figure 2, Figure 3, and Tables S2-S3, screen use is tested as mediator (path a: NDC -> screen use; path b: screen use -> outcome; path c': indirect effect). No analysis exists where a phone/SM variable is itself the IV or DV. Discussion confirms: "measures of screen use showed nonsignificant mediating effects" -- treated throughout as mediator only. Binding rule: SM as mediator-only fails FT-IC2.

notes: EXCLUDE FT-EC2. Screen use (including social media, passive watching) functions exclusively as a candidate mediator in all analyses. The primary IV is the neurodevelopmental spectrum factor; the primary DVs are psychiatric dimensions. Screen use has no standalone analysis as IV or DV anywhere in main or supplementary material.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data (5.0 data release, NDA Collection #2573), N=9,475, baseline ages 9-10 years, 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining predictors of early smartphone ownership age (<10 years) in ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Pediatrics (2026, Vol. 157, Issue 2); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age (early <10 years, binary DV) is a qualifying phone variable. However, the other side consists exclusively of demographic predictors: sex (OR=1.38 female vs. male), race/ethnicity (OR=2.78 Black, OR=1.36 Hispanic, OR=1.69 multiracial), household income (<$75,000 OR=1.10), parental marital status (single parent OR=2.01, cohabiting OR=1.90), and parental education (<bachelor's OR=1.70). All predictors are demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household composition, parental education). Binding rule: demographic-only on the other side of the SM/phone variable -> FT-IC2 NOT_MET regardless of Step 1 qualification of the phone variable itself.

notes: EXCLUDE FT-EC2. The sole analysis pairs smartphone ownership age (qualifying phone DV) with demographic-only predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, income, household structure, parental education). No individual-trait-level variable appears on the other side anywhere in the paper.
